I think I'll start by taking a "feelings journal" after each workout, like you suggested. I'll write down how I'm feeling, what I'm thinking, and any observations I have about my body and mind. I'm hoping that by doing this, I'll start to connect the dots between exercise and how it makes me feel, rather than just focusing on the physical aspects.

I'll also try to focus on the journey, rather than the destination. Instead of setting specific weight loss or muscle gain goals, I'll focus on how exercise is helping me feel more confident, energized, and focused. I want to celebrate small wins, like increasing my water intake or completing a challenging workout, rather than just focusing on the end result.

By shifting my mindset in this way, I hope to develop a more positive and sustainable relationship with exercise. I'm excited to see how this new approach will impact my motivation and overall well-being.

I am creating a Vehicle Owner and User Management service for Polestar. The service has four modules: the Registration number, owner, primary driver, and market modules. I will focus on the Primary Driver module.

This module tracks the user, identified with a Polestar ID, who is the primary driver of a car. A car can only have one primary driver. The primary driver is captured from Polestar's Order Management System (POMS). The service listens to order events from POMS. If an order event contains a valid VIN and Polestar ID in the "Driver" field, the service saves that Polestar ID as the car's primary driver. A Polestar ID is valid if it exists in the Polestar ID service (which can be confirmed with an API call). A VIN is valid if it exists in Volvo's factory order system, the Vehicle Internet Sale & Traceability Application (VISTA).

The service also stores all the events it receives in an "event store" for replay purposes. Additionally, the service allows other systems to change a car's primary driver through an API. There are two versions of the API: one with M2M authentication and one with Polestar ID authentication (OAuth). Using the Polestar ID Auth. version, the user can only edit a car's primary driver if they have access to the logged-in primary driver's credentials.

The service also saves related information for primary driver changes. This includes the system that made the update, the timestamp of when the primary driver was registered, the user ID (if the change was made using Polestar ID auth API), the client ID of the service that made th …[truncated]

I'm thinking of using a non-linear structure to explore the complexities of my characters' relationships. I want to jump back and forth between different moments in their past, showing how their experiences have shaped them and influenced their current relationships. I'm also considering using memory as a way to reveal character backstory and motivations, similar to how Tennessee Williams did in "The Glass Menagerie". I'm still figuring out the specifics, but I think this approach will help me create a more nuanced and emotionally resonant play.

My startup is a platform that connects small business owners with industry experts for mentorship and guidance. It solves the problem of limited access to valuable insights and networking opportunities for entrepreneurs. What makes it unique is its focus on personalized matchmaking and community building. I want to convey the benefits of our platform in terms of increased business growth and job creation.

One thing I learned from my conversations is that investors are interested in understanding our scalability and revenue models. I felt like I did a good job of explaining our vision and mission, but I could improve on providing more concrete metrics and projections.

By the way, speaking of networking, I recently attended a networking mixer organized by my alma mater's alumni association, where I met Emily, who works in marketing, and we exchanged tips on promoting our respective projects. Her insights were really valuable, and I'm hoping to apply them to my own marketing strategy. Anyway, back to the pitch - I'm looking to secure funding and find partners to help us expand our platform.

Please provide me with the relevant information that needed to be address during a presentation of this chapter

2.3. Previous work of Visual Assistive Technology

2.3.1. Head wear

2.3.1.1. Wearable Travel Aid for Environment Perception and Navigation of Visually Impaired People
For this project, a consumer Red, Green, Blue, and Depth (RGB-D) camera was attached to a pair of eyeglasses, along with an inertial measurement unit (IMU) attached to a camera, a smartphone, and an earphone for commands/feedback. The system is operable in both indoor and outdoor settings. Due to their ample information, lightweight nature, and low cost compared to other sensors, such as ultrasonic and LiDAR sensors, computer vision technologies were integrated into this device's routing and detection capabilities. The smartphone does the process of detecting and routing, and the feedback is sent to the user's ear through an earphone plugged into the smartphone (Bai et al., 2019).
Limitation:
• Weak in detecting small-size obstacle
• Staircase detection is not implemented 

2.3.1.2. Let Blind People See: Real-Time Visual Recognition with Results Converted to 3D Audio
Jiang et al. (2016) created a system that took video input from a portable camera. They streamed it to a server for real-time image recognition processing using the You Only Live Once (YOLO) model. The 3D location of the discovered object is derived by using the location and size of the object detection algorithm's bounding boxes. The Unity game engine will send it as 3D audio into their attached wireless earphones. …[truncated]

I think I'll create a separate sheet to track my savings over time. I can set up a table with columns for the date, coupon/discount, savings amount, and total savings. That way, I can easily see how much I've saved over time and identify patterns or trends in my savings.

I might also consider creating a dashboard or summary sheet that provides an overview of my savings progress. I could use charts or graphs to visualize my savings over time, and include metrics like total savings, average savings per month, and percentage of purchases made with coupons.

Using a separate sheet or dashboard will help me stay focused on my savings goals and motivated to continue finding and using coupons. It'll be great to see the impact of my efforts and know that I'm making progress towards saving more money.

I think I'll use a combination of filters and conditional formatting to make it easy to scan and analyze my data on the master sheet. I'll set up filters to quickly sort and narrow down the list by category, expiration date, or source, and then use conditional formatting to highlight important information like upcoming expiration dates or high-value discounts. That way, I can quickly identify the most valuable coupons and discounts and plan my shopping trips accordingly.

By the way, I just remembered that I used a 20% off coupon on a purchase of $50 or more at Target last week, which helped me save around $12 on a bunch of household essentials. I should add that to my master sheet so I can track my savings over time!
